channel.title: Comments for page "Starch and Cream"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Starch and Cream"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm… at a loss for words.

Definitely dig the descent into absurdity that is still gotdam horrifying when you think about it.

Solid +1 for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I…oh my god…this might actually be, in my opinion, the finest specimen of written text to have been produced up to this date. Honestly, it's one world ending problem destroyed by another world ending problem and so on and so forth. Seriously, you kill off a Demonic God by suffocating him WITH DELICIOUSLY SACCHARINE CAKES. You sir have my unwavering and timeless respect. Thank you for this excellence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
THIS ISN'T A WALL OF TEXT. THIS ISN'T EVEN A KNEE-HIGH SHRUBBERY OF TEXT.

I am disappointed.

and will also take your points to heart

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But it's definitely intentional, and plays heavily on the "descent into madness" trope that I like playing with.

But it's not "descent into madness", it's "descent into we stop caring". ;( "Descent into madness" would work quite well, I think, but it's not what's happening here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first section is good, but waaaaay too long before you even have a single line of dialogue. You wanna weave in your exposition with your action/dialogue.

For example:

The Marshall nodded. "And any word from command?"

Sky sighed quietly. There had been no word from command in twenty years. "No, sir. Nothing on the radio."

This stuff is good. This stuff should've been near the beginning, as opposed to pages down.

The scene with the children is pretty adorable.

[22:29] thedeadlymoose I feel like I'm getting mood whiplash from this story
[22:30] thedeadlymoose But I mean like "bored cool awesome bored awesome"
[22:30] thedeadlymoose ^ my reaction so far :P

Kalinin referred to this as "the literary equivalent of taking a shower while someone runs the dishwasher". Yes. (lmao)

The Scarlet King story is pretty 'meh' (and long) until you reach the cakes bit, which was an amazing twist. But then the … potatoes? That could've worked, but it felt a bit like trying too hard to be wacky. Why not just a slowly replicating doomed cakescape, or something? I mean you don't do much with the post-apocalyptic stuff you already have. It's pretty generic, though well-written.

The potato wave goes back to being great, though. As well as the detection systems set up for it.

The ending is really good; goofy but effectively written.

Part Two has a lot of wall-of-text info dumping again, except it's less effective tone-wise than the opening to Part One, while only being slightly more interesting.

Also, when I reached the point where I realized it was definitely a flashback, I felt pretty let down — since I was hoping it wasn't a flashback, because it made the world more interesting. Also, we didn't need to see this at all.

The goat army is trying way too hard to be funny. (Though a few of the individual lines are funny, as is the use of "shitlords".)

Why the Samothrace reference at the ending of the scene? Dull. Overused.

The Blackwood section is redeemed mainly by the amount of fun you're clearly having with it. But it's just not very interesting.

Overall, despite many good bits in here, the 'meh' outweighs the good by quite a lot. :( -1, and I hope you work on it because there's a lot that's good or potentially good here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You are correct. A lot of people have commented on this, and I figure it's about time I make a point to address it.

The tale as a whole is a descent into absurdity, depicted in various ways, each more ridiculous than the one before it.

For example, in the first tale, both the scene and the depiction are dark and aggressive. Only the background is ridiculous.

In the second tale, the scene is serious but both the depiction and the background are ridiculous.

In the final tale, everything is absurd and Blackwood has a fucking entropy gun.

It is a jarring shift between everything, I won't argue this. But it's definitely intentional, and plays heavily on the "descent into madness" trope that I like playing with. It doesn't work for everybody, and that's cool, but it be how it be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. I really do. But damnit, it has an enormous, glaring issue.

The first part of this has a tone vastly different from the rest. It almost feels like they were written as separate tales, and then stapled together. Both of them are very good on their own, don't get me wrong, but the difference between them is jarring.

I'm happy to upvote this, but not until after the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The quality of the writing in this piece is self-evident. I, however, still resent Kaktus for creating a tale that pretty much gave me a case of emotional whiplash by the half of the second act.

Also, Blackwood. Motherfornicating-under-the-consent-of-the-king Blackwood. God I hate that ending. The first act? Awesome. The second? Uh… what? The third? Okay, just stop, you're messing with me.

And still, quality makes it an easy upvote, you BASTARD.

<3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (Is there a way to delete all my comments?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neutral vote. The first portion was absolutely brilliant. This was billed as a silly tale, and the first part of the tale was rooted in silliness, but what made it so good was how genuinely horrifying the world you constructed was. Yes, the idea of infinite cakes having to be nuked from the earth's surface is kind of hilarious, but the way in which it was treated seriously in the writeup and sold by the quality of the writing was excellent. The interplay between the nonsensical setting and the deadly serious events of the story was wonderful. Posted on its own, I would have upvoted enthusiastically.

Act 2 didn't really tell me as a reader anything I didn't really know, and it introduced a lot of goofy author avatar business, which ironically enough is completely true to your stated intent at the top of the page, but completely counter to the spirit of Act 1. A certain degree of thematic whiplash can work when employed well, but this was just too much for me. I could even forgive the silly "piggling" author avatars in the right context (admittedly that context is very narrow), but I don't really see why that stuff needed to be there. It was connected storywise, but didn't really advance the narrative IMO.

And, um, Act 3. I don't know if as a reader of this site I'm ready for more Lord Blackwood material. I'll leave it at that.

It would be a goddamn crime to downvote something as enjoyable as I found the first part of this tale, but I also cannot in good conscience lend my support to the piece as a whole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dammit. I'm feeling like a stick in the mud. :| You lost me with "infinite potatoes are the anti-Keter cakes", then got me again with "Blackwood and Deeds escaped", and then…

It's just, everything's so damned Fallout levels of serious, and then it starts getting silly, and then the ending happens and I just don't know if my irritation was warranted. I mean, recontextualizing the whole thing so the tonal issues make sense works, but then again I also had to endure a few pages' worth of tonal issues before coming to that final scene, which I wasn't expecting in the least.

Ugh, maybe I'm just a butt, I dunno. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+Spoilers
- The Scarlet King says, " Let them eat cake"

So, I read that the Scarlet King died from a cake overdose, and I thought that would be it. Like, ok, death by Keter Cake and a bag of potatoes. Funny, but possible in-universe.

Then you went and did whatever the fuck Parts 2 and 3 are, and left me rolling. Good job, kaktus.

Collapsed edit: I'm also digging the stylistic differences between the sections. Part One mostly reads as a somber post-apocalyptic sci-fi tragedy, while Two was like, Monty Python or Douglas Adams style ridiculousness, and Part Three was the penny dreadful Lord Blackwood we've all come to love. Those shouldn't work together, but they did, and it was beautiful.


I also liked the use of the less-popular author avatars, but I personally would've included McTiriss and Agent Navarro. But I digress. It was an entertaining story.

Uncollapsed edit:

  1. Was the barely-mentioned character 'Max' Max Lombardi? He seriously needs to quit finding himself in these situations.
  2. Stuart Hayward also could've made a good cameo, because everyone knows corpse cats combating Keter cakes is comedic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was amazing. From one keter cake writer to another, I absolutely loved this. Excellent work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Superb. Part 1 was genuinely moving, the rest hilarious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Brilliant. Absolutely brilliant. Anybody can end the world with comets or zombies or plagues or disasters or killer plants or huge green things with teeth. But it takes a special kind of mad bastard to end it with cakes and potatoes.

And I hope that mad bastard wins this thing. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't describe in words the joy I felt while reading this. Well done Kaktus!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a joy to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I burst out laughing multiple times. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You crossed the line so many times, but you ended up on the right side. Wacky and beautiful, I laughed out loud throughout.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So you actually completed it. Well done
This was worth a laugh, or ten.

For absolutely no biased reasons what so ever: +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yessssssssssssss.

Sillypocalypse is bestpocalypse.

item.title: I am so sick of fucking potatoes
item.content:encoded:
Yeah this is silly but sometimes the end of the world is fucking silly so let's do this thing.

I think Conwell looked at this once, so thanks to him.

Good luck, contest folk. May the best potted plant win.
